svg.text: Chunking — slicing docs the right way
Why we chunk, how to choose sizes, and the three strategies you'll actually use
simply won't fit in one embed call.
Smaller chunks let one vector represent
a tight idea instead of an averaged blur
Only the most relevant slices reach the
LLM, preserving its window for thinking
instead of wading through irrelevant text.
one vector for the whole book
meaning is averaged across all chapters
● Single vector blurs many topics together
● Retrieving "the right book" still floods the LLM context
● Search relevance collapses — everything looks similar

g.text: • Too many topics in one vector
Split every N characters. E.g. 250 chars / chunk.
− Cuts off words, splits ideas mid-sentence
Good default for fast prototyping, never for prod alone.
Each chunk shares a tail with the next. E.g. 10% overlap.
+ Words near boundaries appear in 2 chunks → recall↑
10–20% overlap balances recall vs storage well.
Taylor Swift is performing... part of her Eras Tour.
Thousands of fans are traveling... hotel demand spike.
Local hotels are fully booked... prices doubled.
− Chunk sizes vary wildly (very long or very short)
Recursive: tries paragraphs first, then sentences,
then words → until each chunk fits the size limit.

svg.text: 5. Different document types → different split characters

g.text: Split by page or section after

text.tspan: the right splitter knows your format. Wrong splitter = chunks that break mid-tag, mid-function, or mid-table → useless retrieval.

svg.text: Don't reach for a generic text splitter when your data is HTML, code, or structured tables.

g.text: Fixed-size + overlap fits in 5 lines of Python.
For real systems, reach for battle-tested libs:
Every chunk should carry its parent's metadata,
Lets you cite, deduplicate, and rebuild context.

svg.text: ✓ Inherit doc metadata + add chunk index
● Semantic chunking — find natural breakpoints with embeddings
● Contextual Retrieval (Anthropic) — prepend doc context per chunk
● Late chunking (Jina) — embed full doc first, chunk the vectors
● Hierarchical chunks — store parent & child, retrieve at multi-grain
